div.div: Nothing special — just the game board on page 1.

This is essentially Simon Says with a different name. The leader — called the Grand Mufti — makes all kinds of gestures, simple or elaborate. Players must copy only gestures that are preceded by the phrase 'Thus says the Grand Mufti—'. Any gesture made without that formula must be ignored. The Mufti tries to trick players by speeding up, blending formulas, or making convincing motions without the phrase. Anyone who copies a non-formula gesture must do a quick silly penalty — tell a joke, do a spin, sing a line.

Victorian parlor games frequently borrowed exotic names from the Ottoman Empire, Persia, and Egypt. The Grand Mufti was an actual Ottoman religious and legal office — its absolute authority seemed amusingly apt for a game about obedience.

One player is the Grand Mufti and makes various gestures. Players must imitate only those gestures introduced with 'Thus says the Grand Mufti.' If the Mufti makes a gesture without the sacred formula, players must remain still. Those who imitate wrongly pay a forfeit — the oriental-exotic theme being a popular Victorian fancy.

svg.text: Source: The Sociable, or One Thousand and One Home Amusements (1858) · Heritage Parlor
